title: Top Ten Adventure Games - Part One
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pKvwesJ8-t0

Daniel (@dungeondive) tackles what he calls the hardest list yet: top ten adventure games (Part One covers 10-6). Adventure games differ from dungeon crawls in their focus on overland traversal and narrative immersion—exploring kingdoms rather than corridors. Legends of Andor opens at number ten with Michael Menzel's breathtaking art and epic fantasy scope, though its strict timer somewhat constrains the adventure feel. Talisman Second Edition (1983) swings to the opposite spectrum as a narrative sandbox where you create your own story by connecting random encounters—just as important as the game itself.

Explore the Forests of Eternity (formerly called Hexscape) surprisingly jumps to number eight after Daniel initially dismissed it, won over by Tom Chick's passionate review and discovering rich quest variety and narrative encounters. Lost Valley captures adventure through the lens of a Yukon Gold Rush prospector hunting, fishing, and panning—proving that magic and monsters aren't necessary for genuine exploration. Arkham Horror 3rd Edition rounds the bottom five with its refined focus on making Arkham City feel like a real place and time, creating genuine investigative immersion despite Daniel's concerns about Fantasy Flight's post-release support.

----

Which adventure games surprised you by appearing (or not appearing) on Daniel's list, and did his distinction between dungeon crawls and adventure games change how you categorize your own collection?
